But, it does such a poor job of making that information available to the public its overall score in the bottom group of states. While the official Virginia outlet for lobbying information is not up to par, the Virginia Public Access Project, an independent non-profit group, has filled some of the gaps

Oklahoma's guidance states that 'lobbyists should communicate between themselves to ensure each lobbyist registers the principal [client] using the same information and to determine which lobbyist will pay the registration fee for the principal.' Providing reliable and consistent information to the public is not achieved by asking reporting parties to talk amongst themselves

The two defining characteristics of all the top-scoring states (16 and up) is that they each require compensation to be reported and they make that information available to the public in an accessible way

nearly all of the poorest scoring states, those that scored less than 10 total, received a zero for the fact that compensation reporting is not required. These states also tended to have poor scores in the other categories
Seventeen states do not require compensation paid to lobbyists be disclosed at all
Seven more states only require partial reporting with compensation either reported in ranges or not linked to individual lobbyists
This is a standard that 20 states are already using some variation of
16 states do require some form of monthly reporting, often tied to the legislative session
